planting.note: Root stem cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown plants in fall in lean, very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; California fuchsia requires excellent drainage and no summer irrigation once established — it evolved in the Mediterranean climate of California where summer drought is the norm. Cut back hard to 6 inches in late winter to stimulate vigorous new growth for the next bloom cycle. Previously classified as Zauschneria californica.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; California fuchsia (formerly Zauschneria californica) is a California and Baja California native that produces one of the most spectacular late-season, drought-tolerant flowering displays of any western plant — a spreading, somewhat shrubby perennial with small, gray-green to silvery-hairy leaves that from August through November is completely covered in brilliant, intense, scarlet-orange to cardinal-red, tubular flowers, each 1–1.5 inches long and perfectly shaped for hummingbird pollination. The timing of the bloom — late summer through autumn — coincides exactly with the southward migration of the rufous, Anna's, and Allen's hummingbirds along the Pacific Coast, making California fuchsia one of the most important late-season hummingbird nectar sources in the western United States. It thrives in the dry, lean, Mediterranean-climate conditions of its native chaparral and coastal scrub habitat and actually resents summer watering once established. The cultivar 'Everett's Choice' and 'Catalina' are particularly vigorous and floriferous. Outstanding for California native gardens, drought-tolerant western landscaping, and any sunny, lean, dry position where a brilliant late-season display is desired.

planting.note: Root root cuttings (3–4 inch sections of roots placed horizontally in sand) in late winter, or plant container-grown plants in fall in lean, dry, well-drained soil in full sun; matilija poppy resents disturbance and transplants with difficulty — choose a permanent location and do not move it. It spreads by underground runners and can be invasive in moist, fertile soils. Seed germination requires smoke treatment or fire scarification and is difficult; cuttings are more reliable.

directions: Plant in lean, dry, very well-drained soil in full sun; matilija poppy is one of the most dramatic and spectacular flowering perennials of the western United States — a large, subshrubby California and Baja California native producing glaucous, blue-gray, deeply cut foliage on stems reaching 5–8 feet, and from May through July, flowers of extraordinary size and beauty: pure white, papery-petaled, deeply crinkled blooms up to 9 inches across with a large, rounded, golden-yellow cluster of stamens at the center — the largest flowers of any native plant in North America. The size and purity of the white blooms, combined with the fragrant, crepe-paper quality of the petals and the golden center, make each individual flower an extraordinary sight, and a large, established clump in full bloom is a garden spectacle with few rivals. It evolved in the chaparral and canyon walls of southern California where fire triggers germination of the seeds, and it is perfectly adapted to summer drought, lean soil, and Mediterranean conditions. It spreads assertively by underground runners and can colonize large areas — useful for erosion control on dry slopes, but potentially problematic in small gardens.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade to part shade; greater celandine self-sows prolifically and can naturalize aggressively — in some regions it is considered a weed or invasive species (check local regulations before planting). All parts are toxic if ingested; the orange-yellow latex sap causes skin irritation in some individuals.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in shade to part shade; greater celandine is a European and western Asian member of the poppy family that has naturalized extensively in North America in disturbed, shaded situations — old walls, woodland edges, shaded garden corners, and waste ground — where it grows as a short-lived, freely self-sowing perennial producing pale-blue-gray, deeply lobed, softly hairy foliage and small, four-petaled, cheerful-yellow flowers from spring through summer in a nearly continuous display. When the stems or leaves are broken, they exude a bright, intense orange-yellow latex sap that has been used since antiquity to treat skin conditions, particularly warts (the name tetterwort refers to its historical use for skin eruptions). The sap is caustic and can irritate skin and is toxic if ingested. In the garden it is best used as a shade-tolerant naturalizing plant under trees and shrubs where its self-sowing habit creates a tapestry of bluish foliage and yellow flowers without requiring care. The double-flowered cultivar 'Flore Pleno' has attractive pompom-like flowers and is somewhat less freely self-sowing than the species.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds where plants are to grow permanently in spring or fall; yellow horned poppy has a deep taproot and resents transplanting. It is biennial to short-lived perennial — forming a rosette the first year and flowering from the second year onward. It self-sows freely in appropriate conditions. All parts are toxic if ingested. The conspicuously long (6–12 inch) seed pods are the source of the 'horned' common name.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; yellow horned poppy is a coastal biennial or short-lived perennial native to the shingle beaches, sea cliffs, and dry wasteland of the Mediterranean and Atlantic coasts of Europe — one of the most dramatically attractive plants of shingle habitats, producing a large rosette of deeply lobed, wavy-edged, intensely glaucous, blue-gray, rough-textured, almost metallic-appearing leaves in the first year, and from June through September of the second year, a succession of large (2–3 inch), crinkled-petaled, vivid butter-yellow poppy flowers followed by the plant's most extraordinary feature — slender, horn-like seed pods up to 12 inches long that curve and arc dramatically, giving the plant an immediately recognizable, sculptural quality when seeding. The combination of the intensely silver-blue foliage and the bright yellow flowers on a large, architecturally dramatic plant is one of the finest available in dry, coastal or gravel gardens. Self-sows modestly to maintain colonies in appropriate lean, well-drained conditions.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in spring in lean, very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; plains yellow primrose requires excellent drainage and is native to the dry, calcareous soils of the Great Plains and western prairies. It is very drought-tolerant once established but will rot in heavy, wet soils. A good choice for rock gardens, gravel gardens, and dry prairie restorations in the central states.

directions: Plant in lean, sharply drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; plains yellow primrose is a low-growing, spreading native perennial of the calcareous prairies, rocky glades, and dry bluffs of the central Great Plains — from the Dakotas south to Texas and west to the Rocky Mountains — producing narrow, slightly toothed leaves on spreading stems and a profuse display of clear, bright yellow, four-petaled flowers about 1–1.5 inches across from late spring through summer. The flowers have a soft, crepe-paper texture characteristic of the evening primrose family and open in the morning rather than in the evening, despite the family association. An exceptionally drought-tolerant plant of dry, calcareous soil, it is well-adapted to the same difficult conditions as the central Texas Hill Country where limestone-based, thin, droughty soil is the norm. It spreads moderately by both seed and spreading stems to form a low groundcover in dry, open positions. Excellent for the xeric prairie garden, rock garden, and any lean, calcareous soil situation where a low, spreading, yellow-flowered native perennial is needed. Visited by native bees and other pollinators throughout its extended bloom season.
